column6: description
Prefer checkpoints with large marginal contribution to the leave-one-out committee.
Prefer pivotal checkpoints while suppressing redundant ones.
Prefer checkpoints that improve the hardest support examples.
Prefer checkpoints whose residual error pattern cancels other family members.
Prefer checkpoints that both correct the committee residual and oppose the family source-domain residual.
Prefer checkpoints that rescue hard examples without blowing up the worst source domain.
Prefer checkpoints that correct the uniform committee residual directly.
Prefer checkpoints with low second-derivative norm in prediction space.
Prefer accurate checkpoints that are not redundant with highly similar peers.
Prefer checkpoints whose low-rank-denoised projection quality aligns with committee residual correction.
Prefer checkpoints that improve confidence on near-boundary support examples.
Reward hard-example gains while guarding against damage on easy examples.
Prefer checkpoints that narrow the gap between the two hardest source domains instead of specializing in one.
Prefer checkpoints whose removal hurts the uniform committee the most.
Prefer checkpoints that improve boundary examples without large source-domain imbalance.
Prefer checkpoints with balanced calibration across classes.
Prefer checkpoints that sit close to community centers in trajectory space.
Inverse JS distance to the family consensus prediction.
Prefer checkpoints with balanced losses across example-difficulty deciles.
Prefer checkpoints that bridge the early and late ends of the family.
Prefer underrepresented communities while still favoring individually strong members.
Allocate mass across communities, then rank members by community-level validation quality.
Prefer pivotal checkpoints, but penalize source-mixture sensitivity.
Prefer checkpoints far from the graph-diff anchor.
Prefer checkpoints whose source-domain errors cancel the family residual while staying broad across domains.
Prefer high-leverage checkpoints in the principal subspace of the family trajectory.
Prefer checkpoints with low first-derivative norm in prediction space.
Prefer checkpoints that stay cautious when they are wrong.
Prefer checkpoints that move toward the truth where the consensus is wrong.
Prefer checkpoints with low expected calibration error.
Prefer flat low-loss plateau centers instead of arbitrary late points.
Prefer residual-correcting checkpoints that also remain calibrated.
Prefer checkpoints with low Brier score on support labels.
Prefer checkpoints that are correct on the high-disagreement hard examples where the committee struggles.
Prefer checkpoints aligned with the uniform committee residual on support labels.
Consensus barycenter after trimming the most disagreeing support examples.
Prefer checkpoints whose confusion pattern complements the family mean confusion matrix.
Prefer checkpoints that revisit earlier predictive states.
Prefer turning points and inflection points in the support/validation trajectory.
Balance residual correction against redundancy penalties.
Prefer checkpoints that improve boundary examples while staying broad across source domains.
Prefer checkpoints that add predictive novelty without drifting too far too late.
Prefer checkpoints whose local loss trend sign is stable rather than oscillatory.
Prefer checkpoints with balanced domain competence that remain stable under leave-one-domain-out perturbations.
Prefer checkpoints whose per-class residual errors are orthogonal to the family mean.
Equalize mass across early, middle, and late phases before ranking within each phase.
Prefer checkpoints that bridge hard domains while remaining insensitive to source-mixture reweighting.
Prefer checkpoints whose domain-loss vector cancels the family mean domain residual.
Prefer checkpoints whose confusion pattern complements the family while still expanding the family envelope.
Prefer checkpoints that occupy the periphery of their communities.
Prefer checkpoints whose source-domain loss vector decorrelates from the family mean domain residual.
Prefer checkpoints whose source-domain score stays stable under leave-one-domain-out perturbations.
Blend consensus centrality with centroid outlier mass to avoid hub collapse.
Prefer members from tighter, less dispersed four-community neighborhoods.
Prefer checkpoints whose leave-one-out committee stays closest to the full committee.
Prefer checkpoints that stay uncertain when they are wrong, rather than being confidently wrong under shift.
Prefer checkpoints that stay away from their nearest neighbor.
Prefer checkpoints whose source-domain score is stable across different source-mixture weightings.
Prefer checkpoints with useful residuals outside the family low-rank subspace.
Prefer checkpoints far from the rest on average.
Prefer inter-community bridges, but penalize members from poor-quality communities.
Prefer checkpoints occupying rare principal-sign patterns.
Interpolate between medoid representatives and community fringes.
Prefer medoid-like representatives inside the dominant trajectory communities.
Prefer checkpoints with at least one very distant partner.
Prefer checkpoints with balanced source-domain competence rather than one-domain collapse.
Prefer peripheral representatives from the trajectory communities rather than their medoids.
Prefer checkpoints far from the family center along the early-to-late axis.
Prefer checkpoints that sit near the boundaries between trajectory communities.
Prefer checkpoints that are novel relative to the past trajectory.
Prefer the opposite extreme along the first principal trajectory direction.
Prefer checkpoints far from the family centroid.
Prefer checkpoints that expand the family envelope along leading principal axes.
Prefer checkpoints that look unlike what comes after them.
Prefer one extreme along the first principal trajectory direction.
